coachNote: Welcome back whenever you sit down — no guilt about the gap, life happens. I'm holding the same set on purpose: this is the pop session you asked for, and I haven't gotten a rating back yet, so I don't want to change anything until you've actually played it. Whenever you're ready, this is a great one to dive into: I–V–vi–IV in C (C–G–Am–F — "Let It Be," "Don't Stop Believin'"), the 50s reorder (C–Am–F–G, the "Stand By Me" sound), the same shape moved into G, and the broken-chord version that makes it sound like a real song instead of blocks. On G♭, stay with the tiny thumb-under drill — four notes, right hand only, very slow; that one spot is the whole job. Scales and the recall warm-ups are your steady backbone. The one thing I really need back from you: rate honestly, and tell me which of these progressions you recognize from songs you know — that's what tells me where to take the harmony next.

exercises.label: C major — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
C D E F | G A B c |]
exercises.cue: Quick warm-up. Say "C major" out loud, then play it both hands from memory — no fingerings printed.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if truly stuck)

exercises.label: A natural minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F G A |]
exercises.cue: Same white keys as C major — so how do you know it's A minor? It starts and rests on A. Name it, then play it.
exercises.fingering: From memory — RH 1 2 3 1 2 3 4 5 · LH 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1 (only if stuck)

exercises.label: A harmonic minor — hands together, from memory
exercises.block: Recall warm-up · no fingerings on the staff
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:Am
A, B, C D | E F ^G A |]
exercises.cue: One note makes it "harmonic" instead of natural minor — the raised 7th, G♯. Name it, find it, play it from memory.
exercises.fingering: From memory — the only black key is the G♯ near the top

exercises.label: B♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: You hit a 5 last time — let's confirm it's really owned. One notch faster, and watch the 4th finger land clean on the black key.

exercises.label: E♭ major — hands together
exercises.cue: Steady at a 4 — push the tempo a touch. Thumbs find the white keys, long fingers stay home on the black.

exercises.label: A♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Holding at a 4 — keep it crisp. Thumb on C and F; finger 3 starts you.

exercises.label: D♭ major — right hand
exercises.cue: Five flats, holding at 4 — a touch faster. Thumbs on the two white keys (F and C); everything else is black.

exercises.label: G♭ major — thumb-under drill (the spot that breaks)
exercises.cue: Taking it apart hands-separate slipped to a 2 — so we shrink it all the way down. Just four notes, right hand only, very slow: practice the thumb tucking under to land on that white key, up and back. Get THIS automatic and the rest of the scale follows. Left hand rests today.

exercises.label: D natural minor — hands together
exercises.cue: Productive at a 4 — hold it together, nudge the tempo. One flat (B♭), same finger pattern as C in a darker key.

exercises.label: I–V–vi–IV in C — the pop progression (hands together)
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/1
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "C"[CEG] | "G"[GBd] | "Am"[Ace] | "F"[FAc] |]
[V:2] C, | G, | A, | F, |]
exercises.cue: This is the one — C–G–Am–F, the most-played progression in pop. "Let It Be," "Don't Stop Believin'," "Someone Like You," hundreds more. Four plain triads, no sevenths. Right hand plays the chord, left hand names the root. Name each chord before you play it.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 3 5 on each triad · LH single root underneath

exercises.label: I–vi–IV–V in C — the "50s" progression (hands together)
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/1
K:C
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "C"[CEG] | "Am"[Ace] | "F"[FAc] | "G"[GBd] |]
[V:2] C, | A, | F, | G, |]
exercises.cue: Same four triads, new order — C–Am–F–G, the "Stand By Me" / "Earth Angel" sound. Notice how reordering the same chords changes the whole feeling. Hands together, name them as you go.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 3 5 on each triad · LH single root underneath

exercises.label: I–V–vi–IV in G — the same shape, new key (hands together)
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/1
K:G
V:1 clef=treble
V:2 clef=bass
[V:1] "G"[GBd] | "D"[DFA] | "Em"[EGB] | "C"[CEG] |]
[V:2] G, | D, | E, | C, |]
exercises.cue: The same I–V–vi–IV moved into G — a favorite key in pop and on guitar. Two new chords to meet: D major and E minor. The key signature hands you the F♯ automatically, so D plays as D–F♯–A.
exercises.fingering: RH 1 3 5 on each triad · LH single root underneath

exercises.label: Broken-chord pop pattern — I–V–vi–IV in C (right hand)
exercises.abc:
X:1
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
"C"C E G E | "G"G B d B | "Am"A c e c | "F"F A c A |]
exercises.cue: Now make it sound like a song. Same C–G–Am–F, but instead of blocking each chord, roll it — bottom, middle, top, middle. This broken-chord pattern is the backbone of a hundred pop ballads. Keep it smooth and even.
